common_name: Horsehead Philodendron (Oak Leaf Philodendron, Laciniatum Philodendron)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio vine in zone 9; Philodendron pedatum cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. Provide a moss pole or rough climbing surface — the leaf lobing becomes progressively deeper and more dramatic on climbing stems compared to trailing ones. Grows rapidly under good conditions. Propagate from single-node stem cuttings in water or moist mix. Use a well-aerated potting mix. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Horsehead Philodendron grows fastest and develops its most dramatic leaf lobing in consistent bright indirect light, though it tolerates lower light levels than many collector aroids without obvious deterioration. Philodendron pedatum is a climbing philodendron native to Venezuela, Trinidad, Guyana, and northern Brazil, readily identifiable by its uniquely shaped adult leaves — deeply and irregularly lobed into three to five distinct sections in a pattern that, viewed from certain angles, resembles the silhouette of a horse's head (hence 'Horsehead Philodendron') or the lobed shape of an oak leaf (hence 'Oak Leaf Philodendron'). The leaf shape is significantly more complex than most climbing philodendrons: a broad primary lobe is flanked by smaller lateral lobes, and the arrangement of these lobes relative to the petiole attachment creates a distinctive, almost sculptural leaf form that changes in complexity as the plant matures on a climbing support. The leaves are glossy, medium-green, leathery, and held on long petioles. Philodendron pedatum is one of the most available and most adaptable of the lobed-leaf climbing philodendrons, growing more vigorously and tolerating imperfect conditions better than the demanding collector types. It has been used extensively in hybridization, notably as a parent of the popular 'Philodendron Florida Green' and related Florida hybrid series. All parts are toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Philodendron pedatum species notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Philodendron species for indoor culture
American Horticulture Society — Lobed-leaf climbing philodendrons
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Philodendron (toxic to pets)

common_name: Superb Anthurium (Puzzle Anthurium, Bird's Nest Bird's Nest)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or greenhouse specimen in bright indirect light; Anthurium superbum is more adaptable than many collector anthuriums, tolerating lower humidity (though preferring 50–70%) and moderate temperature fluctuation. Use a well-draining, humus-rich potting mix. The rosette growth habit means the plant grows outward rather than upward — allow adequate horizontal space. Propagate from offsets or fresh seed. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in moderate to bright indirect shade — Anthurium superbum is among the most shade-tolerant of the commonly available anthuriums, thriving in lower light conditions where other large Anthuriums would struggle. Anthurium superbum is a large rosette-forming anthurium native to Ecuador, producing a bold, bird's-nest or fountain-like rosette of large, broadly strap-shaped, leathery leaves that arch outward from the center in a vase-like arrangement superficially resembling large Bird's Nest Ferns (Asplenium nidus) or the rosette form of Anthurium hookeri. The leaves are distinguished by their unusual surface texture: the upper surface is covered with irregular, slightly raised areas and depressed zones — a rough, bumpy, puzzle-like texture that gives the species the alternative common name 'Puzzle Anthurium.' The undersides of the leaves are dark violet-purple, creating a striking color contrast visible when the large leaves arch outward. Anthurium superbum produces long flower spadices that develop into dense, elongated clusters of deep purple-to-black berries when fertilized — the berry-laden spadix is itself ornamental. This species is one of the larger-growing of the commonly available bird's nest-type anthuriums, eventually forming an impressive specimen suitable as a floor plant in appropriate indoor spaces. All parts are toxic due to calcium oxalate.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium superbum species notes
Missouri Botanical Garden — Anthurium superbum cultivar profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Anthurium foliage plants for collectors
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to pets)

common_name: Australian Wax Plant (Common Wax Flower, Hoya australis)

planting.note: Grow as a hanging basket or trained vine on a trellis in bright indirect to partial shade; Hoya australis can tolerate brief temperatures to 35°F if the soil is dry — its Australian origin makes it more cold-tolerant than most Hoyas. In zone 9b-10, it can be grown outdoors in a sheltered spot. As with all Hoyas, never remove old flower stalks (peduncles) — new flower clusters emerge from the same peduncle each season. Allow to become slightly pot-bound before repotting. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light to partial shade — Hoya australis blooms most freely in bright indirect to gentle morning sun and will produce few flowers in shade, but performs well as a trailing houseplant even in moderate light. Hoya australis is a vigorous, twining, semi-succulent epiphytic vine native to eastern Australia (Queensland and New South Wales), one of the few Hoyas from the Australian continent rather than Southeast Asia, and one of the most cold-tolerant commonly grown Hoya species. The plant produces long, trailing stems of paired, broadly oval to slightly rounded, fleshy, glossy, dark-green leaves that are thicker and more succulent than most commonly grown Hoyas. In summer (its natural Southern Hemisphere flowering season, now extended to warm months in cultivation), Hoya australis produces large, globe-shaped umbels of 20–40 individual star-shaped flowers — pure white with a faint pink-red corona — with an intensely sweet, musky, almost spicy fragrance that is among the most powerful of any Hoya species. The combination of vigorous growth, good cold tolerance (relative to other Hoyas), reliable blooming, and powerful fragrance makes Hoya australis one of the most recommended starter Hoyas and a valuable addition to zone 9 shaded container and patio displays. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: American Hoya Society — Hoya australis care guide
Australian National Botanic Gardens — Hoya australis (native range notes)
Royal Horticultural Society — Hoya australis cultivation
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Hoya (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Split-Leaf Monstera (Pinnatipartita, Peru Monstera, Broad-Leaf Monstera)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio vine in zone 9; Monstera pinnatipartita cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. Provide a moss pole or climbing support to achieve the dramatically pinnate adult leaf form — the transformation from juvenile simple leaves to deeply split adult leaves is one of the most striking in the genus. In zone 10-11 outdoors, it can climb tree trunks in deep shade. Propagate from stem cuttings with at least one node. Use a well-draining aroid potting mix. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Monstera pinnatipartita transitions from its juvenile to its extraordinary adult leaf form most rapidly with adequate bright indirect light and a climbing support. Monstera pinnatipartita is a climbing aroid from Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia in the Araceae family, producing one of the most dramatically transformative leaf progressions in the genus Monstera — the group named for their 'monstrous' leaves. In the juvenile trailing or hanging phase, the plant produces relatively small (4–6 inch), simple, elongated, glossy dark-green leaves that give no indication of what is to come. As the plant climbs a vertical surface and matures, the leaves transform dramatically: they enlarge significantly and become deeply pinnately lobed — divided almost to the midrib into numerous (8–12 pairs or more) distinct lobes — creating a leaf structure that is deeply slashed rather than fenestrated (holed), with the individual lobes remaining attached to the central rib like a feather. The adult leaves, reaching 24 inches or more, are among the most striking in the genus. Monstera pinnatipartita is frequently confused in commerce with Monstera siltepecana (which is similar in juvenile form), Rhaphidophora decursiva (similar in adult form), and even Epipremnum pinnatum — accurate identification is helpful for appropriate care and expectation setting. All parts are toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Monstera pinnatipartita species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Monstera species for indoor culture
American Horticulture Society — Collector Monstera species identification
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Monstera (toxic to pets)

common_name: Singapore Plumeria (White Frangipani, Evergreen Frangipani, Blunt Leaf Plumeria)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun with excellently drained soil; Plumeria obtusa is the most cold-sensitive of the common plumerias and is best suited to zone 10-11 outdoors or large containers moved inside in zone 9 winters. Unlike P. rubra, it remains evergreen year-round in frost-free climates, making it excellent for tropical streetscapes. Cuttings 12–18 inches long should be dried for 5–7 days before planting in dry, sandy mix. The milky sap causes skin and eye irritation.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, preferably sandy or rocky soil; Plumeria obtusa is drought-tolerant once established and will rot in heavy, moisture-retaining soils. Plumeria obtusa is a tropical flowering tree native to the Caribbean islands (Cuba, Jamaica, Bahamas, Hispaniola) that has become one of the most widely planted ornamental trees throughout tropical Asia, particularly Singapore and Thailand, where it is now so ubiquitous in Buddhist temple gardens and public landscapes that it has acquired the common name 'Singapore Plumeria,' though it is not native to that region. Unlike the deciduous Plumeria rubra (the most widely planted frangipani in the US), P. obtusa remains evergreen year-round in frost-free climates, retaining its broad, glossy, dark-green, blunt-tipped leaves through all seasons — the specific epithet 'obtusa' refers to the rounded, blunt (obtuse) leaf tips that distinguish it from the narrower-tipped P. rubra. The flowers are pure white with a bright yellow center and produce the classic sweet, tropical frangipani fragrance that is among the most widely recognized flower fragrances in the world. The blooms appear at the stem tips in clusters and are borne for months in warm climates. The milky, latex sap that exudes from cut stems is irritating to skin and eyes. In zone 9, P. obtusa is most practical as a large container specimen brought in during winter.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Plumeria obtusa (Singapore Plumeria)
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Plumeria obtusa: evergreen frangipani
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Plumeria species for Gulf Coast zone 9-10
American Plumeria Society — Plumeria obtusa cultivation notes
